module_map.responsibility: Framework internals: request handlers (WSGI/ASGI), exception hierarchy, validators, signals, paginator, signing. The BaseHandler class in core/handlers/base.py owns the middleware chain assembly and request/response dispatch loop.
HTTP primitives: HttpRequest (parsing headers, body, cookies, querystring), HttpResponse hierarchy (streaming, file, JSON), multipart parser. All view-layer data flows through these types.

URL routing: pattern compilation (RegexPattern, RoutePattern), recursive resolution (URLResolver), view-callable lookup, reverse URL generation. Produces ResolverMatch that carries the view callback and captured kwargs.
ORM layer: model definition, QuerySet API, SQL compilation, connection management, transactions, migrations. Central abstraction for all persistence.

Request/response processing pipeline stages: CSRF protection, security headers, caching, GZIP, locale, common redirects, CSP. Each class follows the MiddlewareMixin interface exposing process_request / process_response / process_exception hooks.
View layer: class-based views (View, TemplateView, ListView, etc.), error pages, CSRF error view, static file serving, i18n. Views receive an HttpRequest and return an HttpResponse.
Template engine: parsing, compilation, rendering. Engine registry, Context, Template, Node classes. TemplateResponse lazily renders at response finalization.
Settings management via lazy LazySettings proxy. Global settings defaults in global_settings.py. The settings object is imported everywhere; it is a root dependency of the entire framework.

Shared utility library used across all modules: encoding, HTML escaping, functional helpers (lazy, cached_property), deprecation, async helpers (asyncio.py), logging, datastructures, decorators, crypto, HTTP utilities.
Signal/slot event system (Signal, receiver). Used by models (pre_save, post_save), requests, migrations, and test framework for decoupled inter-module communication.
Application registry (Apps). Tracks installed apps, manages AppConfig instances, drives the ready() lifecycle and model/signal wiring at startup.
Form and field validation layer: Form, ModelForm, Field, Widget, FormSet. Bridges user input (HttpRequest.POST) to validated Python types and model instances.

pipeline_trace.description: WSGI/ASGI server calls WSGIHandler.__call__ or ASGIHandler.__call__. Raw environ dict is converted into an HttpRequest object. Handler inherits BaseHandler.

BaseHandler._middleware_chain is a composed callable built by load_middleware(). Each middleware's process_request hook is called in order. Hooks may short-circuit and return an HttpResponse (e.g. CSRF rejection). adapt_method_mode wraps sync/async mismatches transparently.

BaseHandler.resolve_request calls get_resolver(urlconf).resolve(request.path_info). URLResolver walks the registered URLPattern/URLResolver tree matching patterns. Returns a ResolverMatch containing the view callable, positional args, kwargs, and url_name.

BaseHandler._get_response/_get_response_async calls process_view middlewares, then invokes the resolved callback (function view or View.as_view()). make_view_atomic wraps the callback in a DB transaction if ATOMIC_REQUESTS is set. The callback receives request plus captured URL kwargs.

View logic calls Model.objects.filter(...) or similar QuerySet methods. QuerySet is lazy; SQL is compiled and executed when iterated. The database cursor returns rows that are hydrated back into Model instances.
View returns an HttpResponse or TemplateResponse. TemplateResponse.render() is called lazily before the middleware response phase. Response carries status code, headers, and content.

Each middleware's process_response hook is called in reverse order (LIFO). Middlewares can mutate headers (caching, security, GZIP, CSP). process_exception hooks fire if an unhandled exception propagated.
Final HttpResponse is serialized: status line and headers sent via start_response, body chunked via __iter__. ASGI handler sends http.response.start and http.response.body ASGI events.

cross_module_hubs.reason: Pure utility library imported by every other module. Provides MiddlewareMixin (used by all middleware), functional helpers (lazy, cached_property), encoding, HTML safety types (SafeString), deprecation machinery, async wrappers, and logging helpers. No module in django/ avoids importing utils, making it the most universally depended-upon package.

The global `settings` object (from django.conf import settings) is imported by virtually every module: middleware, ORM, templates, URL routing, validators, forms. It is a singleton dependency root with almost no outbound deps of its own, making it the highest inbound-dependency hub in the codebase.

Defines the central exception hierarchy (ValidationError, ImproperlyConfigured, PermissionDenied, SuspiciousOperation) imported by all other modules, and BaseHandler which integrates urls, db, middleware, and utils into the request pipeline. It is both a heavy importer and a heavily-imported exception/handler source.

change_proposal.feature_summary: Add an async-compatible StructuredLoggingMiddleware that captures per-request metadata (resolved view name, ORM query count, total response time, status code) and emits a single structured JSON log entry after each response.
new_types: StructuredLoggingMiddleware: inherits MiddlewareMixin from django/utils/deprecation.py; sets async_capable=True and sync_capable=True; overrides __call__ to record time.monotonic() before passing to inner handler, then post-response reads request.resolver_match.view_name, len(connection.queries), response.status_code, and emits a JSON-serializable dict via Python stdlib logging.

RequestLogRecord: TypedDict with fields view_name: str, method: str, path: str, status_code: int, query_count: int, response_time_ms: float — constructed in the middleware and passed as the log message extra.
change_proposal.pattern_to_follow: Follow the MiddlewareMixin pattern used by all classes in django/middleware/. The mixin (django/utils/deprecation.py) provides sync/async dual-mode dispatch. For a __call__-override approach (needed for accurate timing wrapping the entire chain), reference CsrfViewMiddleware which overrides __call__ directly while still honoring async_capable/sync_capable flags. For logging conventions, follow django/utils/log.py (AdminEmailHandler, CallbackFilter) and the existing log_response() helper in that module.
change_proposal.integration_point: Placed as the first (outermost) entry in the MIDDLEWARE setting so it wraps the full chain and measures total wall-clock time. The view name is read from request.resolver_match, which BaseHandler.resolve_request populates before view dispatch (core/handlers/base.py:302-317). ORM query count uses django.db.connection.queries (available when DEBUG=True) or a custom db signal counter for production.
risks: ORM query count via connection.queries only works when DEBUG=True; a production-safe alternative requires listening to the django.db.backends signal (execute_wrapper) or a thread/task-local counter, adding non-trivial complexity.
Timing a StreamingHttpResponse is inaccurate because the body is consumed lazily after __call__ returns; the logged response_time_ms will only cover time-to-first-byte, not full transfer time.

In async views, incorrect async_capable / sync_capable configuration or a missing await in __call__ will cause silent synchronous wrapping via async_to_sync, potentially deadlocking under high concurrency in an async server.
Adding the middleware to MIDDLEWARE at position 0 means every request pays the overhead; it should be opt-in and not included in the default MIDDLEWARE list.

JSON serialization of log records in the hot path adds measurable per-request latency at high throughput; the implementation should defer serialization to the logging Handler/Formatter layer rather than doing it inline in the middleware.
